question:
7. Let \( f(x) \) be a function such that \( f(1)=1 \) and for \( x \geq 1 \)
\[
f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{1}{x^{2}+f^{2}(x)}
\]

Prove that
\[
\lim _{x \rightarrow \infty} f(x)
\]
exists and is less than \( 1+\pi / 4 \)
solution:
Solution. Since \( f^{\prime} \) is everywhere positive, \( f \) is strictly increasing and therefore
\[
f(t)>f(1)=1 \quad \text { for } t>1
\]

Therefore
\[
f^{\prime}(t)=\frac{1}{t^{2}+f^{2}(t)}<\frac{1}{t^{2}+1} \quad \text { for } t>1
\]

So
\[
\begin{array}{c}
f(x)=1+\int_{1}^{x} f^{\prime}(t) d t \\
<1+\int_{1}^{x} \frac{1}{1+t^{2}} d t<1+\int_{1}^{\infty} \frac{d t}{1+t^{2}}=1+\pi / 4
\end{array}
\]

Since \( f \) is increasing and bounded, \( \lim _{x \rightarrow \infty} f(x) \) exists and is at most \( 1+\pi / 4 \). Strict inequality also follows from (1) because
\[
\lim _{x \rightarrow \infty} f(x)=1+\int_{1}^{\infty} f^{\prime}(t) d t<1+\int_{1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{1+t^{2}} d t=1+\pi / 4
\]

descriptive_long.question:
7. Let \( growth(abscissa) \) be a function such that \( growth(1)=1 \) and for \( abscissa \geq 1 \)
\[
growth^{\prime}(abscissa)=\frac{1}{abscissa^{2}+growth^{2}(abscissa)}
\]

Prove that
\[
\lim _{abscissa \rightarrow \infty} growth(abscissa)
\]
exists and is less than \( 1+\pi / 4 \)
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. Since \( growth^{\prime} \) is everywhere positive, \( growth \) is strictly increasing and therefore
\[
growth(parameter)>growth(1)=1 \quad \text { for } parameter>1
\]

Therefore
\[
growth^{\prime}(parameter)=\frac{1}{parameter^{2}+growth^{2}(parameter)}<\frac{1}{parameter^{2}+1} \quad \text { for } parameter>1
\]

So
\[
\begin{array}{c}
growth(abscissa)=1+\int_{1}^{abscissa} growth^{\prime}(parameter) \, d\,parameter \\
<1+\int_{1}^{abscissa} \frac{1}{1+parameter^{2}} \, d\,parameter<1+\int_{1}^{\infty} \frac{d\,parameter}{1+parameter^{2}}=1+\pi / 4
\end{array}
\]

Since \( growth \) is increasing and bounded, \( \lim _{abscissa \rightarrow \infty} growth(abscissa) \) exists and is at most \( 1+\pi / 4 \). Strict inequality also follows from (1) because
\[
\lim _{abscissa \rightarrow \infty} growth(abscissa)=1+\int_{1}^{\infty} growth^{\prime}(parameter) \, d\,parameter<1+\int_{1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{1+parameter^{2}} \, d\,parameter=1+\pi / 4
\]

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
7. Let \( sandstone(cloverleaf) \) be a function such that \( sandstone(1)=1 \) and for \( cloverleaf \geq 1 \)
\[
sandstone^{\prime}(cloverleaf)=\frac{1}{cloverleaf^{2}+sandstone^{2}(cloverleaf)}
\]

Prove that
\[
\lim _{cloverleaf \rightarrow \infty} sandstone(cloverleaf)
\]
exists and is less than \( 1+\pi / 4 \)
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. Since \( sandstone^{\prime} \) is everywhere positive, \( sandstone \) is strictly increasing and therefore
\[
sandstone(driftwood)>sandstone(1)=1 \quad \text { for } driftwood>1
\]

Therefore
\[
sandstone^{\prime}(driftwood)=\frac{1}{driftwood^{2}+sandstone^{2}(driftwood)}<\frac{1}{driftwood^{2}+1} \quad \text { for } driftwood>1
\]

So
\[
\begin{array}{c}
sandstone(cloverleaf)=1+\int_{1}^{cloverleaf} sandstone^{\prime}(driftwood) d driftwood \\
<1+\int_{1}^{cloverleaf} \frac{1}{1+driftwood^{2}} d driftwood<1+\int_{1}^{\infty} \frac{d driftwood}{1+driftwood^{2}}=1+\pi / 4
\end{array}
\]

Since \( sandstone \) is increasing and bounded, \( \lim _{cloverleaf \rightarrow \infty} sandstone(cloverleaf) \) exists and is at most \( 1+\pi / 4 \). Strict inequality also follows from (1) because
\[
\lim _{cloverleaf \rightarrow \infty} sandstone(cloverleaf)=1+\int_{1}^{\infty} sandstone^{\prime}(driftwood) d driftwood<1+\int_{1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{1+driftwood^{2}} d driftwood=1+\pi / 4
\]

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
7. Let \( constantmap(fixedpoint) \) be a function such that \( constantmap(1)=1 \) and for \( fixedpoint \geq 1 \)
\[
constantmap^{\prime}(fixedpoint)=\frac{1}{fixedpoint^{2}+constantmap^{2}(fixedpoint)}
\]

Prove that
\[
\lim _{fixedpoint \rightarrow \infty} constantmap(fixedpoint)
\]
exists and is less than \( 1+\pi / 4 \)
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. Since \( constantmap^{\prime} \) is everywhere positive, \( constantmap \) is strictly increasing and therefore
\[
constantmap(distance)>constantmap(1)=1 \quad \text { for } distance>1
\]

Therefore
\[
constantmap^{\prime}(distance)=\frac{1}{distance^{2}+constantmap^{2}(distance)}<\frac{1}{distance^{2}+1} \quad \text { for } distance>1
\]

So
\[
\begin{array}{c}
constantmap(fixedpoint)=1+\int_{1}^{fixedpoint} constantmap^{\prime}(distance) d distance \\
<1+\int_{1}^{fixedpoint} \frac{1}{1+distance^{2}} d distance<1+\int_{1}^{\infty} \frac{d distance}{1+distance^{2}}=1+\pi / 4
\end{array}
\]

Since \( constantmap \) is increasing and bounded, \( \lim _{fixedpoint \rightarrow \infty} constantmap(fixedpoint) \) exists and is at most \( 1+\pi / 4 \). Strict inequality also follows from (1) because
\[
\lim _{fixedpoint \rightarrow \infty} constantmap(fixedpoint)=1+\int_{1}^{\infty} constantmap^{\prime}(distance) d distance<1+\int_{1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{1+distance^{2}} d distance=1+\pi / 4
\]

garbled_string.question:
7. Let \( mqsdlkpa(vprnlogu) \) be a function such that \( mqsdlkpa(1)=1 \) and for \( vprnlogu \geq 1 \)
\[
mqsdlkpa^{\prime}(vprnlogu)=\frac{1}{vprnlogu^{2}+mqsdlkpa^{2}(vprnlogu)}
\]

Prove that
\[
\lim _{vprnlogu \rightarrow \infty} mqsdlkpa(vprnlogu)
\]
exists and is less than \( 1+\pi / 4 \)
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. Since \( mqsdlkpa^{\prime} \) is everywhere positive, \( mqsdlkpa \) is strictly increasing and therefore
\[
mqsdlkpa(chsweorf)>mqsdlkpa(1)=1 \quad \text { for } chsweorf>1
\]

Therefore
\[
mqsdlkpa^{\prime}(chsweorf)=\frac{1}{chsweorf^{2}+mqsdlkpa^{2}(chsweorf)}<\frac{1}{chsweorf^{2}+1} \quad \text { for } chsweorf>1
\]

So
\[
\begin{array}{c}
mqsdlkpa(vprnlogu)=1+\int_{1}^{vprnlogu} mqsdlkpa^{\prime}(chsweorf) d chsweorf \\
<1+\int_{1}^{vprnlogu} \frac{1}{1+chsweorf^{2}} d chsweorf<1+\int_{1}^{\infty} \frac{d chsweorf}{1+chsweorf^{2}}=1+\pi / 4
\end{array}
\]

Since \( mqsdlkpa \) is increasing and bounded, \( \lim _{vprnlogu \rightarrow \infty} mqsdlkpa(vprnlogu) \) exists and is at most \( 1+\pi / 4 \). Strict inequality also follows from (1) because
\[
\lim _{vprnlogu \rightarrow \infty} mqsdlkpa(vprnlogu)=1+\int_{1}^{\infty} mqsdlkpa^{\prime}(chsweorf) d chsweorf<1+\int_{1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{1+chsweorf^{2}} d chsweorf=1+\pi / 4
\]

kernel_variant.question:
Let\(\;f:[2,\infty)\to\mathbb R\) satisfy
\[
 f(2)=\tfrac12\quad\text{and}\quad f'(x)=\frac{2}{x^{3}+f^{2}(x)}\qquad(x\ge 2).
\]
Prove that the limit
\[\displaystyle \lim_{x\to\infty}f(x)\]
exists and that
\[\displaystyle \lim_{x\to\infty}f(x)<\tfrac34.\]
kernel_variant.solution:
Let f:[2,\infty )\to \mathbb{R} satisfy f(2)=\frac{1}{2} and f'(x)=2/(x^3+f^2(x)) for x\geq 2.

1. Since the numerator 2>0 and the denominator x^3+f^2(x)>0, we have f'(x)>0. Hence f is strictly increasing, so for x>2,
   f(x)>f(2)=\frac{1}{2}.

2. Thus for t\geq 2 we get f^2(t)>\frac{1}{4} and
   0<f'(t)=2/(t^3+f^2(t))<2/(t^3+\frac{1}{4}).

3. Integrating from 2 to x\geq 2 gives
   f(x)=f(2)+\int _2x f'(t)dt<\frac{1}{2}+\int _2x2/(t^3+\frac{1}{4})dt<\frac{1}{2}+\int _2x2/t^3dt<\frac{1}{2}+\int _2^\infty 2/t^3dt=\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{4}=\frac{3}{4}.
   Hence f(x)<\frac{3}{4} for all x, so f is bounded above by \frac{3}{4}.

4. Because f is increasing and bounded above, the limit L=lim_x\to \infty f(x) exists.

5. Applying the same integrand estimate over [2,\infty ) yields
   L=\frac{1}{2}+\int _2^\infty f'(t)dt<\frac{1}{2}+\int _2^\infty 2/t^3dt=\frac{3}{4}.

Therefore, lim_x\to \infty f(x) exists and is strictly less than \frac{3}{4}. \blacksquare

core_steps: Observe f′(x)>0 ⇒ f is strictly increasing
Integrate this inequality from the base point to x to obtain an explicit upper bound for f(x)
Show the improper integral ∫ 1/(t²+f(1)²) dt converges, so f is bounded above
Increasing + bounded ⇒ limit exists; strict inequality carried through the ‘<’ in the integral comparison

slot1.description: chosen base point where the initial value of f is given

slot2.description: initial value f(slot1) that supplies a positive constant to compare with f(t)

slot3.description: power on the independent variable in the denominator of f′ (any exponent >1 still gives an integrable comparison function)

slot4.description: positive constant numerator in f′; any fixed positive number keeps f′ positive and merely rescales all bounds

slot5.description: numerical value of the arctangent-based upper bound (comes from integrating 1/(t²+slot2²) from slot1 to ∞)
